title: Timeline
text:
Registration opens Students may register individually or as a team.



Registration Closes All participants must complete registration by this date.



Kickoff + Dataset Introduction
Teams receive the challenge overview and dataset information.



Team Analysis + Office Hours
Teams work on their analyses and receive feedback from faculty, industry professionals, and advanced students.



Write-Up Due
Teams submit a short write-up, maximum 2 pages.



Presentations + Judging
Teams present their findings and answer questions from judges.

title: Judging & Awards
text:
The Dayton Data Challenge is designed to reward thoughtful analysis, meaningful insights, and effective communication.
Judges will evaluate projects holistically. Strong projects do not necessarily use the most advanced methods; rather, they demonstrate curiosity, sound reasoning, and clear communication of findings.

title: Judging & Awards
text:
Judging Criteria
Teams will be evaluated on the quality of their analysis, creativity, communication, and teamwork.


Insight & Relevance
The analysis addresses a meaningful question and provides useful conclusions.


Creativity & Innovation
The team approaches the data in an interesting, thoughtful, or original way.


Clarity of Communication
The findings are explained clearly and effectively.


Technical Soundness
The analysis is appropriate, careful, and well-supported by the data.


Teamwork
The team demonstrates collaboration and shared understanding.

title: Judging & Awards
text:
What Makes a Strong Project?
Strong projects often:

Ask meaningful questions
Use appropriate methods
Present clear visualizations
Communicate findings effectively
Connect results to real-world implications

title: Judging & Awards
text:
Awards
Awards may include:
🥇 First Place
🥈 Second Place
🥉 Third Place
Special awards may include:
🏆 Best Visualization
🏆 Most Creative Approach
🏆 Best Communication
🏆 Best Use of AI / Tools
Participants who complete the competition will receive a certificate of participation.

title: FAQ
text:
Any undergraduate student at the University of Dayton may participate. Students from any major and any experience level are welcome.



No. Prior experience in statistics, programming, or data analytics is not required. Curiosity and willingness to learn are enough.



No. You may register with a team or register individually. Individual participants may request help with team matching.



Teams should have 2–4 students.



Teams may use tools of their choice, including R, Python, Excel, Tableau, Power BI, or AI-assisted tools.



Yes, but teams are responsible for understanding, explaining, and verifying their work. AI tools should support your analysis, not replace your reasoning.



The dataset will be introduced at the kickoff session on Monday, November 2 at 6 PM.



Each team submits a written summary, maximum 2 pages, by Friday, November 6 at 11 PM, and gives a short presentation on Saturday, November 7.



Presentations are expected to be approximately 5 minutes, followed by Q&A with judges.

title: Competition
text:
The Dayton Data Challenge is open to all undergraduate students at the University of Dayton.
Students from any major and any experience level are encouraged to participate.
No prior experience in data analytics, statistics, programming, or competitions is required.

title: Competition
text:
The Dayton Data Challenge is open to all undergraduate students at the University of Dayton.
Students from any major and any experience level are encouraged to participate.
No prior experience in data analytics, statistics, programming, or competitions is required.

title: Competition
text:
Team Format

Teams of 2-4 students
Students may form their own teams
Individual sign-ups are welcome; we will help match students with teams

title: Competition
text:
How It Works
Unlike many competitions, participants are not asked to solve a predefined problem.
Instead, teams develop their own questions, explore the dataset from their own perspective, and communicate their findings through a written summary and presentation.

Form a team or sign up individually.
Attend the kickoff and dataset introduction.
Choose a research question that interests your team.
Analyze the data using tools of your choice.
Attend office hours for feedback and support.
Submit a short write-up.
Present your findings to judges.

title: Competition
text:
Deliverables


Write-Up
Due Friday at 11 PM. Maximum 2 pages. Summarize your research question, method, and findings.


Presentation
Saturday. Approximately 5 minutes plus Q&A. Focus on clear communication and meaningful insights.

title: Competition
text:
Judging Focus
Projects will be evaluated based on:

Insight and relevance
Creativity and innovation
Communication
Technical soundness
Teamwork

See the Judging & Awards page for additional details.

title: Competition
text:
Tools and Resources
Students may use any tools appropriate for their analysis, including:

R
Python
Excel
Tableau
Power BI
AI-assisted tools

Participants are responsible for understanding and explaining their work, regardless of the tools used.

title: About
text:
The Dayton Data Challenge is a week-long undergraduate data competition at the University of Dayton. Students work in teams to analyze real-world data, develop their own research questions, and present insights to judges.
The event is hosted by the Department of Mathematics with support from the Data Analytics Club.

title: About
text:
The Dayton Data Challenge is a week-long undergraduate data competition at the University of Dayton. Students work in teams to analyze real-world data, develop their own research questions, and present insights to judges.
The event is hosted by the Department of Mathematics with support from the Data Analytics Club.

title: About
text:
What Makes It Different?
Participants choose their own questions and may use the tools they prefer, including R, Python, Excel, or other data analysis tools.
The challenge focuses on:


Insight
What did you learn from the data, and why does it matter?


Creativity
How did your team approach the problem in an interesting or original way?


Communication
Can your team explain the story clearly to a broad audience?



Ask Questions • Explore Data • Communicate Insights

Unlike many competitions, participants are not evaluated on whether they reach a predetermined answer. Instead, teams are encouraged to ask meaningful questions, explore the data thoughtfully, and communicate their findings effectively.

title: Dayton Data Challenge
text:
About the Challenge
The Dayton Data Challenge is a week-long data competition at the University of Dayton. Students work in teams of 2–4, analyze real-world datasets, develop their own research questions, and present their insights to judges.

title: Dayton Data Challenge
text:
Why Participate?


Real-World Experience
Work with authentic datasets and practice turning data into decisions.


Team-Based Learning
Collaborate with students from different majors and experience levels.


Professional Communication
Present insights clearly to faculty, industry professionals, and judges.


Resume Building
Earn experience, recognition, and a certificate of participation.

title: Dayton Data Challenge
text:

All undergraduate students
